title: Victoria sandwich, the classic
subtitle: Two 20 cm sponges, raspberry jam, a snow of icing sugar
excerpt: The British layer cake at its plainest. Equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, egg; a 20 cm tin; twenty minutes in the oven. Sandwich with raspberry jam, dust with icing sugar, serve with strong tea.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The four-quarters method (equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, eggs) was already the household standard. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, gives the earliest weighted British cake recipes in wide circulation. The cake takes its name from Queen Victoria, whose afternoon tea is the cited 1860s origin; the recipe predates the name by decades.

content.text: Two sponges baked in 20 cm tins, sandwiched with raspberry jam, dusted with icing sugar. Equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, eggs, the four-quarters method that household cookbooks have used since Acton's 1845 weighting. Twenty minutes from bowl to oven; twenty-two minutes in the oven; the cake is ready for tea in under an hour.

Plan for everything at room temperature before you start. Cold butter doesn't cream; cold eggs split the batter. Leave the butter on the worktop for an hour before you begin and the work goes smoothly.

attrs.body: Weigh the eggs in their shells; that's the reference weight. Whatever they come to, take the same weight in butter, sugar and self-raising flour. Four medium eggs come to roughly 200 g, which is what the recipe below uses. If your eggs come to 215 g, take 215 g of each.
attrs.title: The four-quarters rule

content.text: What you need

Two 20 cm loose-bottomed tins are the standard. A 23 cm tin works for both sponges baked separately, or one deeper cake split in half; the bake time stretches by about five minutes. Grease the tins and line the bases with circles of baking paper.
Method
Cream the butter and sugar, 5 minutes

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Put {{unsalted-butter}} of softened unsalted butter and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a large bowl. Beat with a hand mixer on medium-high for four to five minutes (the
creaming method
); the mixture goes from yellow and dense to pale and almost double in volume. This is the cake's main rise; the air you whip in here is what holds the crumb open.
Add the eggs gradually

Lightly beat {{eggs}} eggs in a jug. With the mixer running on medium, add the eggs a tablespoon at a time, letting each addition emulsify into the butter before the next. If the mixture looks curdled, add a heaped tablespoon of the weighed flour and beat for ten seconds. It brings the emulsion back. Beat in {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract.
Fold in the flour

Sift {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour and {{baking-powder}} of baking powder over the bowl. Fold in with a large spatula in a figure-of-eight motion: cut down through the centre of the batter, scrape the bottom, lift over the top. Eight to ten strokes is usually enough. Stop when the flour streaks have disappeared; another stroke past that point loses the air you whipped in.
Divide and bake, 22 minutes

Weigh the batter into the two prepared tins, eyeballing the split is the single biggest cause of uneven sponges. Each tin takes around 350 g. Smooth the surface of each tin gently with the back of the spatula. Bake on the middle shelf for 20 to 22 minutes. The sponges are done when the tops spring back to a fingertip touch, the edges have pulled away from the tin, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean.
Cool, 30 minutes

Leave the tins on a rack for 10 minutes. Run a palette knife around the edge of each tin and turn the sponges out onto the rack, paper still attached. Peel the paper off when the sponges are cool enough to handle. Cool completely (at least 20 more minutes) before sandwiching. A warm sponge melts the jam and slides apart.
Sandwich and dust

Sit one sponge flat-side-up on a serving plate. Spread {{jam}} of raspberry jam over the surface, leaving a 1 cm border at the edge. Settle the second sponge on top, flat-side-down. Dust {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar through a fine sieve over the surface. The classic finish (no buttercream, no fresh cream) keeps the cake plain.
Troubleshooting

attrs.intro: The five ways a Victoria sandwich goes wrong, and what to fix next time.

items.fix: Stop folding the moment the flour streaks disappear. Don't open the oven door before the 18-minute mark.
items.cause: Over-mixed batter or oven opened mid-bake.

items.fix: Four to five minutes is the minimum. Look for pale and almost double in volume, not just combined.
items.cause: Butter and sugar weren't creamed long enough, not enough air whipped in.

items.fix: Weigh the tins on a scale; aim for 350 g per tin.
items.cause: Batter split by eye, not by weight.

items.fix: Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more, an oven thermometer takes the guessing out. If yours runs hot, drop to 170°C and add 2 to 3 minutes.
items.symptom: Sponges peaked or cracked on top

items.fix: The skewer should come out clean but the sponge should still spring back. If the skewer is bone-dry, the cake has gone two minutes too long; pull at the first clean skewer next time.

attrs.heading: If something went sideways

content.text: Variations

A lemon Victoria takes the zest of two unwaxed lemons folded into the creamed butter, and lemon curd instead of raspberry jam. A buttercream version adds vanilla buttercream as well as the jam. Soften 100 g unsalted butter. Beat in 200 g sifted icing sugar and 1 tsp vanilla. Spread between the jam and the second sponge. The classic four-quarters with raspberry and icing sugar is the cake this recipe is named for. Anything richer is a different cake.
Where this cake lives

The four-quarters cake is older than its royal namesake. Acton's 1845 cookbook gives the same weights and method without calling it a Victoria; Beeton's 1861 edition has it under "plain cakes". The name stuck after Queen Victoria's afternoon tea adopted a version with raspberry jam in the 1860s, and the cake settled into the form it still takes: two equal sponges, jam, no cream, no fuss.
